evals.prompt: Design an innovation center for a 15,000-person retailer. They want to compete with DTC brands.
evals.expected_output: Should run 'what protects what' diagnostic first — likely answer: protecting H3 bets and learning velocity from quarterly comparable-sales pressure. Recommend hybrid archetype: Discovery Lab + Customer Experience Center (not Venture Studio — DTC-style competition isn't separate-business-line, it's category re-imagining). Charter: (1) Mandate — 'Innovate the customer experience that bridges retail and DTC, defending market position against direct-to-consumer challengers'; (2) Scope IN: customer journey reimagining, new digital experiences, in-store/online integration, brand-extension experiments. OUT: ops cost-out, traditional merchandising, supply chain (those belong to ops); (3) Funding — $20-30M annually, 3-year commitment; (4) Decision rights — lab can ship pilots up to $2M; larger requires steering; (5) Talent — 60/40 permanent/rotational, 25-40 people total; (6) KPIs Year 1: capability (talent, partnerships, customer-research depth), 4-6 validated insights, 2-3 pilots launched; Year 2: 1-2 pilots advancing to launch, ecosystem partnerships, customer NPS lift in pilot segments; Year 3+: revenue contribution from launched bets, sustained capability; (7) Sponsor — Chief Customer Officer (likely better than CTO/CDO for this mandate); (8) Sunset — 18mo with no advanced bets triggers re-charter. Pair with innovation-strategist for mandate ratification, innovation-disruption-analyst for DTC threat-mapping.

evals.prompt: Our lab is in year 2 and the CFO wants to kill it. Build the recovery / continuation case.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose first (don't jump to defense), then build the case. Diagnostic questions: (1) What were the original Year-1 KPIs and have they been hit? (2) What capability has been built that wasn't there before? (3) How many bets have advanced through gates? (4) How many BUs have absorbed lab output? (5) Is the original sponsor still in role? (6) What's the lab's visibility — quarterly drumbeat of artifacts? Likely findings: KPI mismatch (revenue expectations on lab work that needed capability metrics), thin output cadence, sponsor turnover or attention drift, weak BU absorber relationships. Recovery plan structure: (1) Re-charter — re-ratify mandate with current sponsor (or new sponsor); reset KPIs to year-1-appropriate (capability) for next 12 months; (2) Visible drumbeat — commit to quarterly outputs (whitepaper, prototype, customer story); (3) Absorber relationships — name 2 operating BUs as committed absorbers; structure handoff protocol; (4) Sponsor succession — name successor sponsor in writing, build that relationship; (5) Quick win — identify a 60-90 day deliverable that produces visible customer impact; (6) CFO conversation — translate lab output into language CFO understands (capability $, market presence, defensive value, learning ROI; not revenue at year 2). Cross-link with innovation-leadership-coach for sponsor and capital work.

evals.prompt: We want a venture studio inside our holding company. Design the charter and operating model.
evals.expected_output: Should structure venture studio charter: (1) Mandate — 'Build new businesses outside the operating perimeter of existing portfolio companies'; (2) Archetype — pure Venture Studio, NOT hybrid; the studio's value is independence from operating businesses; (3) Funding — separate vehicle, multi-year commitment ($30-50M minimum for credibility), staged investment per business; (4) Operating model — small core team (8-15) plus dedicated build teams per active business; venture-style staffing not corporate; (5) Decision rights — studio leads decide bet selection within strategic guardrails; major capital exposed to holding-co governance; (6) P&L — separate; ventures track on venture metrics not corporate metrics; (7) Talent — 70-80% market-comp talent, 20-30% rotational from holding companies for cross-pollination; (8) Path to scale — incubate; spin out, sell, or absorb based on strategic fit; predefine the path; (9) Sunset — generally don't sunset venture studios, but do sunset individual ventures rigorously; if studio underperforms 5 years, reassess; (10) Brand — separate brand from holding company in market; ventures can use the brand as legitimating signal. Pair with consulting-management-consultant for legal structure (LLC, sub-board, etc.) and consulting-sow-risk-advisor for inter-company contracts.

evals.prompt: Diagnose this failed lab post-mortem. The lab ran 28 months, produced 12 prototypes, $0 revenue, lab leader left after sponsor turnover, team dispersed back to operating BUs. What were the structural causes?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose against the year-2 collapse pattern: (1) KPI mismatch — likely revenue or 'commercialization' KPIs applied at year 2 when capability metrics were appropriate; (2) Sponsor exit without succession — sponsor turnover with no successor sponsor cultivated in advance; (3) Capability not absorbed — 12 prototypes, no operating BU absorbers committed; lab became cul-de-sac; (4) Visibility gap — likely no quarterly drumbeat, leadership stopped seeing the work; (5) Talent reabsorption pattern — when sponsor exits and capability isn't visible, talent is pulled back to operating BUs (the easy political move); (6) Charter quality — was sunset criteria explicit? If not, the lab existed by inertia until politically convenient to kill. Lessons for next attempt: (a) multi-year funding committed in writing, not verbally; (b) successor sponsor named at charter signing; (c) ≥2 operating BUs commit as absorbers; (d) capability KPIs in year 1, advancement in year 2, outcomes in year 3+; (e) quarterly visibility drumbeat is non-negotiable; (f) talent rotation designed (not pure-permanent priesthood, not all-rotational with no continuity); (g) sunset criteria explicit so death is honest, not political. Structural causes are nearly always charter-quality plus year-2 collapse patterns. Almost never about idea quality.

evals.prompt: Compare a hub-and-spoke distributed lab vs. a co-located center for a 50-person team across 4 cities.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose use case before recommending. Hub-and-spoke: works for Centers of Excellence and Discovery Labs serving distributed BUs; preserves geographic proximity to operating customers; risk: dilution of lab culture, harder to maintain craft standards. Co-located: works for Venture Studios and tight-collaboration creative work; concentrates talent and culture; risk: distance from operating customers, recruitment limited to talent willing to relocate. For a 50-person team across 4 cities, the question is what kind of work the lab does: (a) If lab serves distributed clients/BUs and runs many parallel discovery projects → hub-and-spoke with strong central craft standards; (b) If lab is venture studio building new businesses → co-locate (one city, top talent, density matters); (c) If lab is CX center with physical demonstration component → co-locate near key customer base; (d) If hybrid Discovery + Venture work → hub-and-spoke with annual co-location summit and quarterly in-person rituals. Recommendation given context: hub-and-spoke with deliberate cultural mechanisms — quarterly all-hands, weekly virtual rituals (demo day, kill ceremony, Brain Trust), shared craft standards documented and enforced, rotating people across hubs. Watch for the failure mode where 'distributed' becomes 'siloed' — explicit cross-hub work assignments prevent that.

evals.prompt: Apply the Ten Faces of Innovation to staff our 25-person innovation lab. We have 8 product designers, 6 engineers, 4 researchers, 3 PMs, 2 strategists, 1 storyteller, 1 director.
evals.expected_output: Should run Ten Faces coverage analysis: (1) Anthropologist — covered by 4 researchers, well-staffed; (2) Experimenter — covered by 6 engineers + 8 designers prototyping; well-staffed; (3) Cross-Pollinator — likely under-staffed; researchers and strategists may play this role partially; recommend designating 1-2 explicit Cross-Pollinator seats with cross-industry input; (4) Hurdler — likely UNDER-COVERED; PMs may play this partially but Hurdler is a distinct skill; recommend hiring or designating 1-2 dedicated Hurdlers (often senior people with deep org navigation); (5) Collaborator — PMs play this; well-covered; (6) Director — 1 director, adequate for 25-person lab; (7) Experience Architect — designers play this; well-covered; (8) Set Designer — likely UNDER-COVERED; recommend designating an Operations/Studio role to own the physical/digital environment; (9) Caregiver — varies by lab type; if customer-facing pilots are running, who delivers? Likely gap; (10) Storyteller — 1, light for a lab producing meaningful output; recommend 2-3 Storyteller-equivalent roles (could be split across communications, demo design, narrative documentation). Major gaps: Hurdler, Set Designer, Storyteller depth. Recommendation: don't simply hire more designers/engineers; rebalance toward operating roles. Reference Kelley pattern that most failed labs over-index on Anthropologist + Experimenter and under-staff Hurdler + Storyteller.

evals.prompt: Our lab has been running 18 months. Sponsor is asking 'when will this make money?' What do I tell them?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the sponsor relationship before crafting the answer. Likely diagnosis: KPI mismatch surfacing at month 18 (the year-2 collapse pattern). Sponsor wasn't aligned to year-1 capability metrics in writing, and now is reverting to revenue expectations. Response strategy: (1) Open with the framing — 'Lab year 1 was capability building. Year 2 is bets advancing. Revenue is appropriately year 3+ for the kind of work we set up to do.' If charter doesn't explicitly say this, that's the structural problem; (2) Show year-1 capability built — talent depth, IP, partnerships, customer relationships, validated insights, bets advanced through gates; quantify in $ where possible (e.g., 'we'd spend $X to acquire this externally'); (3) Show year-2 trajectory — bets currently between Gate 1 and Gate 3, expected outcomes in 12 months; (4) Surface what would change the trajectory — funding stability, BU absorption, sponsor backing for Gate 3 launch decisions; (5) Re-establish KPI agreement in writing — what we measure in year 2, what in year 3; (6) If sponsor isn't satisfied, the lab is at year-2 collapse risk regardless of substance. Cross-link with innovation-leadership-coach: this is fundamentally an innovation-capital and sponsor-management problem, not a metrics problem. Build innovation capital with the sponsor; build successor sponsor relationship; produce a vivid demo or customer story for the next steering.

evals.prompt: We've been told to copy [Pixar / Skunk Works / Google X]. Build the org-specific version, not a clone.
evals.expected_output: Should resist copying directly and surface the structural conditions that made each work. Pixar: small studio (~1,000 people), single creative product (animated film), mechanism-rich culture (Brain Trust, Notes Day, Postmortem), founder-protected, 25 years to build the culture. Skunk Works: classified projects, dedicated perimeter, government funding, top-talent draft, urgent national-security mandate. Google X: separate budget, founder-supported, willing-to-fail mandate, 'moonshot factory' brand, ambient innovation culture in parent company. None of these can be copied directly because the conditions don't transfer. Recommend: (1) name the structural conditions of the target — what made Pixar/Skunk/Google X work; (2) audit your org for which conditions exist or could exist — leadership backing? Multi-year funding? Talent draft? Mandate clarity? Culture mechanisms?; (3) Design a lab that fits YOUR conditions, with deliberate borrowed mechanisms — Brain Trust ritual borrowed from Pixar, talent-density borrowed from Skunk, willing-to-fail mandate from Google X; (4) Build mechanisms not imitate aesthetics — the lab's open kitchen and ping-pong table aren't what makes it work; the rituals, decision rights, and KPIs are. Output: org-specific lab design with explicit named borrowed mechanisms, not copy. Pair with innovation-leadership-coach for cultural mechanism design.
